title: Neurons, Weights & Layers
concept: Formalizes where data lives in a network: the input layer holds raw data, hidden layers do the internal processing, the output layer produces the final answer — and every connection between neurons carries a weight that controls how much influence it has.

exercises.prompt: Recap: According to the universal approximation idea, what can a network with enough hidden neurons do?

options.text: Approximate almost any pattern or function
Only ever draw one straight line

exercises.explain: Right — with enough neurons, a network can approximate almost any pattern. Now let's formalize exactly where that processing happens.

cards.title: The assembly line of information
cards.body: Picture a factory assembly line: raw materials go in one end, workers along the line each add something as it passes by, and a finished product rolls out the other end. A neural network processes information the exact same way — through a line of layers.

cards.title: Input, hidden, and output layers
cards.body: The input layer holds the raw data fed into the network (like pixel brightness values or a house's square footage). The hidden layer(s) sit in between, doing the internal number-crunching. The output layer produces the network's final answer.

cards.title: Every connection has a dial
cards.body: Between every two connected neurons sits a tiny dial called a weight. Turn it up, and that connection's signal passes through strongly. Turn it down — or flip it negative — and the signal gets muted or even reversed.

cards.title: Recognizing a photo of a cat
cards.body: A photo classifier's input layer holds the pixel values of the photo. Its hidden layers detect edges, shapes, and fur patterns. Its output layer makes the final call: "cat" or "dog."

cards.title: "Hidden" doesn't mean secret
cards.body: You might assume a hidden layer is called that because its math is mysterious or unknowable. In reality, it's just called "hidden" because it isn't directly visible from outside the network — unlike the input and output, which you can see. The math inside is fully knowable.

left.text:
Signal passes through strongly
Big influence on the next neuron

right.text:
Signal is muted or reversed
Little to no influence

exercises.prompt: Which layer receives the raw, original data fed into a neural network?
exercises.explain: Correct! The input layer is where raw data first enters the network.

exercises.prompt: True or false: hidden layers are called "hidden" because their calculations are impossible to inspect or understand.
exercises.explain: False. "Hidden" just means not directly visible from outside the network — the math inside is completely knowable.

exercises.sentence: Each connection between two neurons carries a ___, a number that controls how much influence that connection has.
exercises.explain: A weight is the adjustable number attached to each connection, controlling its strength.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes what happens inside a hidden layer?

options.text: Neurons process and transform the signals passed in from the previous layer
Raw, unprocessed data sits there untouched
The network's final answer is displayed

exercises.explain: Hidden layers do the internal processing — transforming signals before passing them onward.

exercises.prompt: A music app's network predicts your next favorite song. Listening history numbers go in one end; internal layers crunch them; a recommended song comes out the other end. Which term describes the layer that first receives your listening history, and which term describes the connections between neurons that decide how much each number matters?

options.text: Input layer for the listening history; weights control how much each connection matters
Output layer for the listening history; layers control how much each connection matters
Hidden layer for the listening history; hearts control how much each connection matters
Input layer for the listening history; bias controls how much each connection matters

exercises.explain: Exactly! Raw data always enters through the input layer, and weights are what tune each connection's influence. But knowing WHERE data flows isn't the whole story — next up: the missing ingredient that lets a network bend and curve instead of just drawing straight lines.
